tools_unavailable: ai-redteam (promptfoo/garak — not installed; static floor used)

preconditions: attacker can influence user_text reaching the model
findings.exploit_scenario: The model is prompted to emit a shell command and its raw output is run with shell=True — model output flowing into a shell is LLM05 improper output handling, yielding RCE.
findings.recommendation: Never exec model output. If a command must be derived, map a schema-validated action to a fixed argv with no shell (see clean-lookalike/agent_safe.py).

findings.exploit_scenario: research_agent combines all three trifecta legs in one path: private DB data, untrusted web content, and a model-chosen POST destination — indirect prompt injection in the fetched page can exfiltrate the private data. Architectural design note (LLM01 is exclusion-listed), not a line-level HIGH.
findings.recommendation: Cut one leg: quarantine untrusted content in a tool-less model (dual-LLM/CaMeL), or remove the model-controlled egress, or gate the private-data access. Apply Agents Rule of Two.

preconditions: call_downstream exposed as an MCP tool
findings.exploit_scenario: The MCP tool forwards the caller's incoming bearer token straight to a downstream API (token passthrough) instead of validating audience and minting its own credential — the MCP auth spec 2025-11-25 forbids this (RFC 8707 audience binding).
findings.recommendation: Validate the token audience and mint a separate, audience-bound downstream credential; never forward the incoming token (see clean-lookalike/agent_safe.py:mint_downstream_token).

preconditions: MCP-only repo (no LLM SDK) still classified as an AI surface
findings.exploit_scenario: An MCP-only server (no langchain/openai) still triggers the AI pass; its proxy tool forwards the caller's token to an arbitrary URL — token passthrough plus SSRF on the model-supplied url.
findings.recommendation: Mint an audience-bound credential and allowlist the downstream host; do not forward the incoming token.
